content.method: Multilevel regression linking county-level SNI/BTMN rates to specialist-teacher-to-pupil ratios, expert-committee waiting times, and sociodemographic composition; supplement with case-file audits in a stratified sample of high- and low-prevalence counties to compare diagnostic thresholds applied to similar presenting cases.
content.question: Does regional variation in Hungary's SNI/BTMN prevalence (5.4%-14% across counties, e.g., Békés vs Borsod-Abaúj-Zemplén) reflect genuine population differences, or differences in diagnostic/administrative capacity, expert-committee practice, and specialist availability?
content.why_it_matters: If variation is capacity-driven rather than epidemiological, then network-planning and workforce-expansion proposals (T5) are more urgent and better targeted than categorical or finance reforms, and current 'high-prevalence' regions may simply have better detection rather than more need.
